channel.title: NYT Book Reviews (Filtered)
channel.description: Actual book reviews from the New York Times, filtered from the noisy Books RSS feed.

item.title: ‘Country of Lords,’ by Kim Phillips-Fein
item.description: In a new book, the historian Kim Phillips-Fein chronicles a “shadow tradition” of anti-egalitarians who fought against America’s founding ideals.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘Dad, Love, Me: A Memoir,’ by Matthew Quick
item.description: Matthew Quick’s memoir takes the form of a letter to his father as dementia slowly dismantles the man who once terrified him.
item.author: Tas Tobey

item.title: ‘The Mortons,’ by Justine Larbalestier and Scott Westerfeld
item.description: In “The Mortons,” a young hit woman — a prodigy at killing — is tested by inherited secrets and her own morality.
item.author: Sarah Lyall

item.title: ‘The Frenchmen,’ by Emily Eakin
item.description: In “The Frenchmen,” Emily Eakin traces the rise and fall of postmodernist philosophy, and the surprising ways its influence lingers.
item.author: Clancy Martin

item.title: ‘Bitchy!,’ by Roberta Gregory
item.description: A new compendium of Roberta Gregory’s comics does away with delicacy in the name of feminism.
item.author: Sam Thielman

item.title: ‘Unsayable,’ by Michael Cunningham
item.description: Blending autobiography with meditations on craft, “Unsayable” feels destined for the graduation gift bag.
item.author: Brandon Taylor

item.title: ‘Cool Machine,’ by Colson Whitehead
item.description: The final installment of his Harlem crime trilogy is full of heists, cultural shifts and nostalgia for 1980s New York.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘The Lost Founder,’ by Jesse Wegman
item.description: In “The Lost Founder,” the journalist Jesse Wegman looks back at a largely forgotten and singular voice that helped make the United States what it is today.
item.author: Claire Rydell Arcenas

item.title: ‘Yellow Pine,’ by Claire Vaye Watkins
item.description: In Claire Vaye Watkins’s novel “Yellow Pine,” a single woman having an existential crisis decides now is the time to have a child.
item.author: Lauren Christensen

item.title: ‘I Want You to Be Happy,’ by Jem Calder
item.description: Jem Calder’s novel, “I Want You to Be Happy,” follows the flailing situationship between two aspiring writers with a 12-year age gap.
item.author: Lizzy Harding

item.title: ‘Journey to Nowhere,’ by Shiva Naipaul
item.description: In “Journey to Nowhere,” Shiva Naipaul reports on the aftermath of a cult’s disastrous turn away from idealism.
item.author: Anderson Tepper

item.title: ‘They Stole a City,’ by Lauren Collins
item.description: A new book by the journalist Lauren Collins chronicles the ongoing effects of the violent takeover of Wilmington, N.C., on those living in its aftermath.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘Air,’ by Christian Kracht
item.description: In “Air,” by the Swiss writer Christian Kracht, a decorator’s unusual commission leads abruptly into another universe.
item.author: Charlie Lee

item.title: ‘Cloudthief,’ by Nathaniel Rich
item.description: “Cloudthief,” by Nathaniel Rich, follows a dispirited writer into a scheme to get rich and find romance along the way.
item.author: Peter C. Baker

item.title: ‘Famous Men,’ by Julie Buntin
item.description: Julie Buntin’s novel “Famous Men” complicates an old story with questions of ambition and consent.
item.author: Lauren Christensen

item.title: ‘The Gospel According to Hobby Lobby,’ by Michael Blanding
item.description: A new book by Michael Blanding chronicles the history of Hobby Lobby and its billionaire founder’s campaign to promote conservative Christian values.
item.author: Virginia Heffernan

item.title: ‘Aging Out,’ by Lucy Schiller
item.description: From assisted living to elder activism and her own experience as a caregiver, Lucy Schiller investigates aging’s myriad challenges in her first book.
item.author: Michelle Orange

item.title: ‘It Will Come Back to You,’ by Sigrid Nunez
item.description: Her first collection of short fiction, “It Will Come Back to You,” is threaded with erudition but never pretentious.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘Catch the Devil,’ by Pamela Colloff
item.description: In “Catch the Devil,” Pamela Colloff vividly tells the true story of the con man who traded on other prisoners’ “confessions” for clemency.
item.author: Ted Conover

item.title: ‘Every Inch a Lady,’ by Audrey Smaltz
item.description: In “Every Inch a Lady,” Audrey Smaltz shares her incredible story — if not her inimitable voice.
item.author: Roxanne Fequiere

item.title: ‘Fixer Chao,’ by Han Ong
item.description: Han Ong’s barbed, irreverent portrait of identity, class envy and flimflam deception in turn-of-the-21st-century Manhattan still resonates.
item.author: Leah Greenblatt

item.title: ‘A Vast Horizon,’ by Anna Thomasson
item.description: A group of artists gathered at a hotel on the Côte d’Azur in 1937. A new book by Anna Thomasson captures the art and escapades the holiday inspired.
item.author: Emily Eakin

item.title: ‘A Table for Fortune,’ by William T. Vollmann
item.description: William T. Vollmann’s fascinating, wildly ambitious text interweaves a family saga with a history of the American war machine since Vietnam.
item.author: Tom LeClair

item.title: ‘You Won’t Get Free of It,’ by Rachel Aviv
item.description: Rachel Aviv’s essay collection, “You Won’t Get Free of It,” shows the breadth and complexity of matrilineal relationships.
item.author: Melanie Thernstrom

item.title: ‘City of Fortune,’ by Mason B. Williams
item.description: A new book by the historian Mason B. Williams chronicles the dubious effects of school, housing and police reforms by local politicians on the right.
item.author: Jacob Heilbrunn

item.title: ‘The Simp,’ by Roshan Sethi
item.description: “The Simp,” by Roshan Sethi, is a satire of the unending demands, learned helplessness and stubborn racism of Hollywood elites.
item.author: Sam Lipsyte

item.title: ‘The Coast of Everything,’ by Guillermo Stitch
item.description: “The Coast of Everything,” by Guillermo Stitch, is structured like a nesting doll, with one story hidden within another.
item.author: Dustin Illingworth

item.title: ‘Men in Love,’ by Irvine Welsh
item.description: Irvine Welsh brings back his beloved Edinburgh ensemble for his new novel, “Men in Love.”
item.author: James Lasdun

item.title: ‘Country People,’ by Daniel Mason
item.description: In Daniel Mason’s charming “Country People,” a stuck father and his happier family are tempted, and changed, during a stint on a bucolic campus.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘American Trickster,’ by Ru Marshall
item.description: In “American Trickster,” Ru Marshall takes on the epic story of Carlos Castaneda.
item.author: Kurt Andersen

item.title: ‘Gerontocracy in America,’ by Samuel Moyn
item.description: A new book by Samuel Moyn argues that the country has become an “oldigarchy,” in which wealth and power are hoarded by the elderly.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘The Midnight Special,’ by Colin Asher
item.description: “The Midnight Special” offers an unconventional cultural history with profiles of five artists influenced by the carceral system.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘Dead but Dreaming of Electric Sheep,’ by Paul Tremblay
item.description: In Paul Tremblay’s new A.I. dystopia, “Dead but Dreaming of Electric Sheep,” an experimental technology gives its heroine control over somebody else’s body.
item.author: Emily C. Hughes

item.title: ‘Becoming George,’ by Fiona Sampson
item.description: In her biography of the novelist George Sand, Fiona Sampson urges us to see Sand chiefly as a writer, an inventor of stories and also of the self.
item.author: Rebecca Chao

item.title: ‘The Yahoo Boys,’ by Carlos Barragán
item.description: A new book examines the desperate lives of Nigeria’s romance scam artists — and their victims.
item.author: Nicolas Niarchos

item.title: ‘Lifeguard,’ by Janet Fash with Clio Chang
item.description: Janet Fash’s memoir is both a sunny coming-of-age story and an exposé of corruption, understaffing and unnecessary deaths at Rockaway Beach.
item.author: Carolyn Kormann

item.title: “Nebraska,” by Monica Datta
item.description: Monica Datta’s “Nebraska” is a maximalist, continent-spanning story of a mother killing her youngest child, as relayed by a highly idiosyncratic psychoanalyst.
item.author: Joumana Khatib

item.title: ‘The Housewives Underground,’ by Kaitlyn Tiffany
item.description: In “The Housewives Underground,” the Atlantic writer Kaitlyn Tiffany salutes a loose network of skeptics who questioned the findings of the Warren Report.
item.author: James Wolcott

item.title: ‘The Emergency Playbook,’ by Amy Edelman and Chris Begley
item.description: “The Emergency Playbook” is a disaster preparation guide that emphasizes community rather than lone-hero fantasies.
item.author: Dwight Garner
